title: Alkyl quinolones mediate heterogeneous colony biofilm architecture that improves community-level survival

supplementary materials.content: Supplemental figures and tables - jb.00095-24-s0001.docxFigures S1 to S8 and Tables S1 to S3.Download8.31 MB

ASM does not own the copyrights to Supplemental Material that may be linked to, or accessed through, an article. The authors have granted ASM a non-exclusive, world-wide license to publish the Supplemental Material files. Please contact the corresponding author directly for reuse.

INTRODUCTION.content: Pseudomonas aeruginosais an opportunistic pathogen and a leading cause of hospital-acquired infections.P. aeruginosareadily forms biofilm communities in many growth environments, where cells live within a self-produced matrix that limits antimicrobial sensitivity (1,2). However, the biofilms formed byP. aeruginosaand other bacteria are not homogeneous. Spatiotemporal heterogeneity arises, for example, through gradients of nutrients consumed and wastes excreted by the attached biofilm cells (3). Furthermore, diversity within the population can help microbes adapt to shifts in their microenvironments (4).

The heterocyclic aromatic 2-alkyl-4(1H)-quinolones (AQs) are a class of secreted molecules produced byP. aeruginosaand other Pseudomonads that can act as antimicrobials as well as environmental signals at the species and interkingdom levels (5,6).P. aeruginosaproduces several AQs, the production of which increases upon surface attachment (7,8). Three commonly identified AQs made byP. aeruginosaare 2-heptyl-4-hydroxyquinoline-N-oxide (HQNO) which inhibits bacterial respiration and causes cell death and extracellular DNA (eDNA) release (9), 4-hydroxy-2-heptylquinolone (HHQ), and 2-heptyl-3-hydroxy-4-quinolone, also namedPseudomonasQuinolone Signal (PQS) which is preceded biosynthetically by HHQ. PQS binds to iron and facilitates the acquisition of iron inP. aeruginosacells while limiting iron access for competing species (10). The AQs HHQ and PQS are signals of the PQS quorum sensing cascade—these AQ congeners can bind to PqsR and upregulate the PQS pathway leading to an increase in their production which, in turn, leads to an increase in virulence factors such as pyocyanin (11–13). Another important class of secreted factors, the rhamnolipids, are produced byP. aeruginosaunder the Rhl quorum sensing cascade and are known to contribute to biofilm and surface spreading phenotypes—rhamnolipids also increase the solubility and bioactivity of AQs (14).

Here we report on the substantial spatial and temporal heterogeneity within a single colony, monoculture,P. aeruginosabiofilms grown on nutrient agar. We find that AQs are essential to the spatial and chemical heterogeneity that develops in theseP. aeruginosabiofilm communities. Using non-invasive, label-free confocal Raman microscopy (CRM) chemical imaging we studied the distribution of AQs (PQS, HQNO, and HHQ) within colony biofilms over time and discovered that AQ chemical signals are spatially dynamic, being strongly associated with aggregates, while being undetectable in adjacent regions, thus creating a heterogeneous patchwork of microenvironments. We characterized three aggregate types, based on size and principal locations, that were observed regularly in the biofilms. The PQS pathway was found to be necessary for the formation of all three classes of aggregates. The largest of these aggregate types was strongly associated with localized regions of cell death, contributing to a colony exhibiting fewer dead cells overall than a colony devoid of AQs.

RESULTS AND DISCUSSION.content: We inspected colonies growing on Petri plates and found that each P. aeruginosa colony growing on agar exhibits spatial heterogeneity across its diameter, even at distances spanning several millimeters. We became intrigued by the highly consistent spatial features apparent in select regions of these colony biofilms ( Fig. 1 ). Attributes can be reliably binned by considering breakpoints along a radial axis starting at the central point of inoculation. By 24 hours of incubation, the presence or absence of numerous aggregate features discernable amongst the biofilm cells, visible by brightfield microscopy made it possible to distinguish among distinct sections of these biofilms ( Fig. 1 ). We considered these structures to be acellular aggregates, different from previously studied cellular aggregates, or clusters of cells adhered to one another ( 15 ). Fig 1 Fig 1 Colony biofilm spatial heterogeneity. Inspection of a 24-hour P . aeruginosa colony expressing green fluorescent protein (green) overlaid with a brightfield microscopy image illustrates the radial heterogeneity of a colony biofilm. Regions lacking green (circled in magenta) are void of cells. Aggregates of diverse shapes (examples circled in orange) are seen as opaque areas of the biofilm. The scale bar is 500 µm. We observed four distinct, spatially segregated regions of these colony biofilms ( Fig. 2 ): an Interior region (0–4 mm from the inoculation point), a Ring region surrounding the Interior having a width of ~1 mm visible to the naked eye, an Outer region beyond the ring (5–14 mm), and a Periphery region (<1 mm) containing the outermost cells. The biofilm Interior harbors a large number of aggregates that are much larger than single cells, while the Ring displays no aggregates. The Outer region has small aggregates roughly the size of 1–3 cells, and the Periphery lacks apparent aggregate structures. Inspection at multiple time points indicates that the Interior and Ring regions are established within the first 24 hours, and subsequent expansion of the colony occurs outside of the Ring. After 48 hours, biofilms were roughly 30 mm in diameter and consistently formed a Ring ~4 mm from the colony center, dividing the colony into an Interior and Outer region. We subsequently inspected these regions using complementary imaging approaches, for example, CRM, brightfield imaging, and fluorescence imaging at multiple scales, to examine equivalent samples prepared to feature specific attributes. Fig 2 Fig 2 Colony biofilms exhibit regions with distinct spatial features and AQ abundance. Colony biofilms imaged at 24, 48, and 72 hours are annotated for their distinctive features and AQ abundance profiles along a radial axis from the point of inoculation to the colony edge. The scale bar is 10 mm. Because AQs have previously been associated with surface-growing P. aeruginosa , we specifically targeted this chemical class and surveyed AQs in different regions of colony biofilms using CRM chemical imaging ( 16 , 17 ). CRM is particularly useful in this context because various features characteristic of HHQ, HQNO, and PQS at 24, 48, and 72 hours can be observed both in the Raman maps and in the principal component analysis (PCA) loading (Z-score) plots and heat maps, see Fig. S1 to S3. HHQ was present at 24 hours in both the Interior and Outer regions, though not detected within the Ring itself ( Fig. 2 ). As expected, PQS was only detected at later times—lagging behind the appearance of HHQ in the Interior region, which also contained HQNO at 48 hours. HHQ was not identified beyond 24 hours in any area of the colony. This may result from HHQ features being obscured by more abundant HQNO and PQS. HQNO was prevalent in the Ring region at 24 hours and found throughout the Interior, Ring, and Outer regions at later times, while the Periphery of the biofilm remained void of detectable AQ signals. Differences in the spatial distribution of HQNO and PQS in our data are consistent with previous observations that the PqsH- and PqsL-dependent pathway branches of AQ synthesis are differentially controlled ( 18 ). While HQNO is often associated with potential antimicrobial properties against competitor species, inhibiting cellular respiration, promoting cell dormancy as well as sensitizing competitors to classes of antibiotics ( 19 , 20 ), we did not find HQNO beyond the colony periphery in the absence of a competitor in these biofilms. We infer that HQNO action is self-directed toward one or more regions of these P. aeruginosa biofilms. For example, HQNO within a P. aeruginosa community can be involved in cell autolysis ( 9 ). Previous work found that in the presence of competitors, HQNO distribution is relatively unchanged, while PQS becomes more prevalent at the P. aeruginosa/ competitor interface ( 21 ). Thus, these colony biofilm results provide additional support for a distinct role of HQNO within P. aeruginosa communities. These four colony biofilm regions are further differentiated by the presence/absence of several large-scale structures when viewed at higher magnification ( Fig. 3 ). We characterized three distinct types of recurrent aggregates. Large aggregate structures were observed in the Interior region (purple arrow in Fig. 3a ). These Large amorphic aggregates were typically >100 µm in diameter and exhibited no consistent shape(s). Their spatial positioning was variable in x–y space with approximately 3–5 aggregates per mm 2 present within the Interior, and their z-axis position was always at the biofilm-air interface. Medium aggregates were radially symmetric structures, ~50 µm in diameter that were typically observed 100–200 µm below the biofilm surface of the Interior (green arrows in Fig. 3a ). Beyond the Ring, the Outer region harbored Small aggregates that were generally ~3–5 µm diameter in size (still larger than single cells). These Small 3–5 µm spherical structures exhibited heterogeneous spacing, with some being found in dense patches, while others were more sparsely distributed. Small aggregates that were found to be sparsely distributed were decorated with crystalline “rays” that protruded outward, resulting in a starlike appearance ( Fig. 3b ). All three—Large, Medium, and Small-aggregate types were present at each time point—24, 48, and 72 hours. After characterizing these aggregates by size and spatial positioning, we explored their properties with respect to chemical composition, cell viability, and secretion of additional soluble factors, for example, cell-free DNA. Fig 3 Fig 3 Aggregates of P. aeruginosa colony biofilms. Three distinct aggregate types, distinguished by size, are present in the Interior and Outer regions. ( a ) The Interior contains Large, >100 µm aggregates (purple arrow and outline), that develop at the biofilm-air interface, while Medium, ~50 µm aggregates (green arrow and circles), develop below the agar surface (imaged here using brightfield). ( b ) Small aggregates (black circles) of ~3–5 µm found in the Outer region readily stained with Nile red, indicating the presence of abundant lipids (shown here by the overlay of brightfield with red fluorescence to image Nile red). Non-lipid “rays” decorate some Small aggregates. Scale bars are 100 µm for these representative images obtained from 48-hour biofilms.

We further characterized these biofilms and the aggregates within them by examining staining patterns with two separate dyes: propidium iodide (PI) and Nile red. PI exhibits fluorescence when bound to double-stranded DNA but does not permeate cell membranes, so it binds to cell-free DNA, making it a well-accepted indicator of cell death ( 22 ). Nile red is commonly used in histology to visualize lipids ( 23 ), which our group has previously used to stain rhamnolipid-rich regions ( 24 ). Typically, the inner portion of Large aggregates showed signatures of localized cell death visualized by propidium iodide fluorescence ( Fig. 4a through c ). While these colony biofilms are replete with live green fluorescent protein (GFP)-expressing cells, the Large aggregates harbored interior puncta of dead cells. Strikingly, these Large aggregates were concurrently devoid of GFP signal (i.e., absence of live cells), as early as 24 hours. In addition, the Ring region was marked by strong propidium iodide fluorescence, indicating an area of continuous cell death encircling the colony Interior ( Fig. 4d and e ); similar to images obtained in prior work examining Bacillus subtilis colony biofilms ( 25 ). In some replicates, protrusions of extracellular DNA, associated with Large aggregates present at the intersection of the Interior and Ring regions, could be seen extending into the Outer region ( Fig. 4f ). Otherwise, little to no cell death was detected outside of the Ring region. Fig 4 Fig 4 Large (>100 µm) aggregates are associated with localized cell death. ( a ) Large aggregates occupy spaces in the Interior region devoid of live cells, as indicated by the lack of green fluorescence from GFP-labeled cells. ( b ) Large aggregates stained with PI (magenta), indicating the presence of extracellular DNA and cell death, are localized to the aggregate center. ( c ) Overlay of fluorescence images a and b. Scale bars are 100 µm. ( d ) Staining with PI illustrates the ring of cell death surrounding the colony biofilm Interior in a fluorescent overlay of a brightfield image. ( e ) Green fluorescence corresponds to image 4d in which cells producing a constitutive fluorescence signal (green) are absent in Large aggregates. ( f ) Large, >100 µm, aggregates found in the region of cell death at the Ring are associated with protrusions of extracellular DNA that extend into the Outer region. Scale bars are 100 µm ( a–c and f ) and 500 µm ( d and e ). Staining with Nile red revealed that the outer portions of the Large aggregates were enriched in lipids (Fig. S4), while the centers, where cell death is apparent, were relatively lipid-poor. The Medium aggregates in the Interior exhibited lipids throughout (Fig. S5a through c), as did the Small aggregates of the Outer region ( Fig. 3b ), though the rays decorating some of these Small aggregates did not exhibit a lipid signature. To validate the crystalline nature of these rays and other aggregate features we examined these regions for birefringence, a characteristic indicator of crystal formation ( 26 ), accompanied by condensation and phase separation ( 27 ). Substantial birefringence was observed throughout the Large and Medium aggregates and in rays of the Small aggregates ( Fig. 5 ). This abundant multi-color birefringence indicates (i) the presence of crystalline features within these aggregates and (ii) that multiple crystalline geometries exist. Condensates are important in biological systems because they differentiate regions and can concentrate factors in beneficial ways ( 28 ). In contrast, the biofilm Periphery region exhibited no DNA, lipid, or crystalline signatures (Fig. S5d and e). Fig 5 Fig 5 Polarized light microscopy highlights crystalline regions associated with aggregates. Non-polarized, brightfield (left) compared to polarized (right) microscopy of aggregates. Regions characterized by polarized light indicate birefringence and the presence of crystalline structures associated with ( a and b ) Large and Medium aggregates of the Interior, ( c and d ) Medium aggregates alone from the Interior, and ( e and f ) Small aggregates from Outer region. Images b and f are compiled (Full focus) images from a Z-series to capture multiple planes simultaneously. Scale bars are 200 µm.

A natural question arises regarding how the AQ signatures are associated with aggregates and cell death. Aggregate structures within P. aeruginosa communities have previously been associated with the AQs HHQ, PQS, and HQNO ( 16 , 17 , 29 ). AQs are hydrophobic and therefore do not diffuse freely through a biofilm, however, the spatial distribution of this class of hydrophobic, extracellular compounds within a colony biofilm is poorly understood. The PQS pathway can promote cell death by promoting pyocyanin production and subsequent H 2 O 2 -mediated lysis ( 30 ), or by the production of reactive oxygenated species from disruption of the electron transport chain by HQNO ( 9 ). Recently, Giallonardi et al. showed that the alkyl quinolone HHQ promotes phage-mediated autolysis in P. aeruginosa colony biofilms ( 31 ). To explore the association of these molecules with aggregate structures at the microscopic level, we examined CRM images to obtain additional chemical insight into these aggregate structures. AQs were associated with a variety of structures within the biofilm. In the Interior region of the colony biofilm, Large aggregates ( Fig. 3b and d ) were associated with HHQ at 24 hours ( Fig. S1 ; Table S1) and HHQ, HQNO, and PQS at 48 hours, with HHQ and PQS most prevalent (Fig. S2; Table S1). Outside of these aggregates, AQs fell below the detection limit of CRM, supporting that these aggregates spatially sequester AQs. CRM images could not be reliably acquired for the Medium structures, also within the colony Interior, due to their depth (typically 100–200 μm below the colony biofilm surface). However, the Outer region exhibited HHQ, PQS, and HQNO features associated with Small aggregates (Fig. S3; Table S1). The crystalline nature of the Large aggregates is consistent with features used to define biomolecular condensates—self-assembled, spatially organized molecules that form under biological stress conditions and are associated with phase separation. Biomolecular condensates, such as stress granules, processing bodies (P-bodies), and bacterial ribonucleoprotein (RNP) bodies (BR-bodies), are known to differentiate the cytoplasmic space of eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells in the absence of a membrane and can facilitate storage of key metabolites and mRNA degradation ( 32 – 34 ). Many biomolecular condensates represent a shift in biomolecular architecture that accompanies stress, such as reduced nutrients or ATP ( 35 ). The aggregates observed here are markedly larger than the previously described biomolecular condensates (~50–200 nm diameter), but their phase shift and ability to spatially differentiate biofilms are similarities worth noting. However, while the importance of small molecules in forming condensates is undeniable, the scales at which these condensates can form are currently unclear ( 36 , 37 ). Interestingly, both Large and Small aggregates contain the same AQs (HHQ, HQNO, and PQS), yet size, shape, birefringent properties, and function differ. Most importantly, Large aggregates are strongly associated with cell death, while Small aggregates are not. Protrusions of extracellular DNA from Large aggregates intersecting the Ring region are associated with regions devoid of cells ( Fig. 1 and 4e ). The spatial arrangement of Large aggregates with an outward-associated plume of eDNA at the Inner-Ring intersection, and cell-void pockets within the Interior, resemble patterns of biofilm dispersion ( 38 ). AQ-rich aggregates create “walls,” as described by Purevdorj-Gage et al., that surround a void containing eDNA but lacking cells ( 39 , 40 ). Dispersion events are also known to be spatially localized and enhanced by the presence of PQS ( 41 ).

We probed the importance of AQs in aggregate formation and associated cell death by comparing the behavior in biofilms formed from the wild type with those from a ΔpqsA isogenic mutant, which cannot produce any AQs. Using brightfield microscopy we find that the ΔpqsA strain is devoid of aggregates and crystalline structures ( Fig. 6a and b ; Fig. S6). We note that a Ring region is still present in the ΔpqsA strain, which is apparent by a discontinuity in cell density extending out from the center. Importantly, complementation of the ΔpqsA mutant in trans restores aggregate formation throughout the biofilm colony ( Fig. 6c ). However, an altered phenotype was observed concomitant with constitutively expressed PqsA such that Large, Medium, and Small aggregates formed by the wild type are not recapitulated with this strain (Table S2) ( Fig. 6a and c ). We associate the differences in patterning between wild type and PqsA overexpression vectors with the importance of native pqsA transcription and the subsequent complex regulation of the entire PQS pathway ( 42 , 43 ). Surprisingly, overall cell death increased in the Δ pqsA colony compared to wild type at 48 hours ( Fig. 7 ). While measurable cell death was present in both the Interior and Outer regions of the Δ pqsA colony, the characteristic localized pockets of cell death and protrusions of eDNA observed in the wild type ( Fig. 7a ) were absent ( Fig. 7b ). This observation is consistent with previous work in which pockets of autolysis and dispersion were observed to be dependent on AQs of the PQS pathway ( 41 , 44 – 46 ). The Δ pqsA colonies exhibit a more homogeneous level of cell death throughout the entire colony ( Fig. 7b ), and overall the Δ pqsA exhibits 2.2× more cell death than the wild type ( Fig. 7d ). This result is consistent with the beneficial roles of bacterial autolysis in biofilm formation through horizontal gene transfer, the elimination of damaged cells, and dispersal of nutrients to surviving cells ( 47 , 48 ). Fig 6 Fig 6 The PQS pathway is required for aggregate formation. Brightfield images show aggregates seen in ( a ) wild type that are absent in ( b ) a pqsA mutant. While aggregate formation is restored in ( c ) the complemented mutant, the size and distribution of aggregates achieved using the overexpression vector still varies from wild type. Scale bars represent 1,000 µm. Fig 7 Fig 7 Overall cell death is increased in the absence of AQs. Brightfield + fluorescent overlay of cell death (magenta; by PI staining) of representative biofilm colonies for (a) wild type, ( b ) Δ pqsA lacking AQs, and (c) Δ pqsA+pqsA complementation strain, all strains being imaged at 48 hours. ( d ) Quantification of cell death by fluorescence per unit area from PI staining for images in a, b, and c. Inset plots for a, b, and c show the distribution of fluorescence intensity over the entire image. Scale bars represent 1 mm. ** P < 0.001, * P < 0.05. The biofilm formed from the complement exhibits an intermediate phenotype ( Fig. 7c ), highlighting the importance of PQS pathway regulation, since the wild-type phenotype is not fully recapitulated by only overexpressing PqsA in trans . The pqsA complement colony exhibited 90% cell death of Δ pqsA , which is still substantively more cell death than the wild-type colony ( Fig. 7d ). Further quantification of images via a histogram of fluorescence intensities over the entire image confirms the stark differences in cell death for these biofilms. As expected, all three colonies have a preponderance of pixels with low fluorescence intensity; however, the Δ pqsA colony exhibits thousands of pixels with higher intensity, consistent with the higher overall level of cell death. HHQ has been found to promote Pf4 phage-mediated cell death ( 31 ). Additionally, Secor et al. identified that Pf4 bacteriophage exhibits birefringence ( 49 ). We tested the possibility that HHQ present in the AQ-rich aggregates might be linked with Pf4 phage to promote localized regions of phage-mediated cell death. We examined colony biofilms of an isogenic ΔintF4 mutant strain, which lacks the integrase necessary for Pf4 bacteriophage excision—this strain is a lytic deficient mutant ( 50 , 51 ). Biofilms for the ΔintF4 were indistinguishable from wild type by the production of aggregates, levels of cell death, and regions of biofilm clearing (Fig. S7) and birefringence (Fig. S6). Thus we judge that Pf4 phage-mediated actions do not influence these features within the timeframe we examined in our experiments. Extracellular DNA is an important structural component of P. aeruginosa biofilms ( 52 – 54 ). The Δ pqsA colonies were of comparable size to wild-type colonies. Following a detergent wash, the area of fluorescence was not significantly different between the Δ pqsA mutant and wild-type colonies given the same treatment (Fig. S8), indicating similar strengths of surface attachment for both strains. This observation is in contrast to flow cell chamber experiments, where eliminating the PQS pathway led to a reduction in extracellular DNA, attributed to prophage induction by PQS, and increased susceptibility to detergent wash ( 55 ). We note that the colony biofilms studied here have no source of fluid exchange or flow. Thus, while flow cells supply liquid media and produce classic mushroom-shaped structures associated with dispersal ( 39 , 40 , 56 , 57 ), colony biofilm communities are >10× larger and survive at the solid-air interface with limited hydration, slowed diffusion, no turbulence, and no fluid stabilized structures.

Colony biofilms are defined by four spatially heterogeneous regions marked by temporal AQ abundance, distinctive aggregate structures, and patterns of cell death. The Inner region contains HHQ at 24 hours and HQNO and PQS at later time points. This 4 mm diameter (at ≥24 hours) Inner region harbors two of the canonical aggregate types identified in this study: Medium aggregates of ~50 µm diameter and Large aggregates of >100 µm diameter. A ~1 mm Ring region surrounds the Inner region and is characterized by its consistent eDNA signature indicating cell death and a prevalence of HQNO at all time points. The Outer region surrounds the Ring. While the Inner and Ring regions maintain a constant size, the Outer region expands from 5 mm to 14 mm in diameter from 24 to 72 hours and is marked by the prevalence of HQNO. PQS is also detected in the Outer region at 72 hours. Also, the Small aggregates of ~3–5 µm are present in the Outer region. Lastly, the Periphery region of roughly 1 mm diameter consistently surrounds the Outer region and is characterized by an absence of aggregates, cell death, or AQ signatures. Small ~3–5 µm spherical aggregates stained positive for lipids, and all AQ classes (HQNO, HHQ, and PQS) were identified in these aggregates. These Small aggregates located in the Outer region were sometimes decorated with rays—crystalline rod-like structures protruding outward from the spherical aggregates. AQs were not identified in these crystalline rays. Thus, the presence of AQs is not always associated with the crystalline structures noted in this study. Both spherical aggregates and rays were absent in biofilms lacking AQs, i.e., the Δ pqsA mutant. The spherical structures observed in the wild-type colony biofilms are consistent with the Outer region promoting the formation of outer membrane vesicles, where lipid bilayers encapsulate AQs and thus participate in their transmembrane trafficking ( 58 ). Medium, ~50 µm, aggregates were located in the biofilm Interior below the surface (100–200 μm deep), making it difficult to obtain structurally detailed chemical images. These structures contained lipids and were also birefringent, suggesting the presence of crystalline domains within these aggregates. While chemical imaging could not be performed, we presume these aggregates also contain AQs, as no such 50 µm structures were observed in a Δ pqsA strain lacking AQs. Large, >100 µm, aggregates were located in the biofilm Interior at the biofilm-air interface. These structures were typically amorphous in shape. Chemical imaging established that HHQ, HQNO, and PQS were each associated with these aggregates, but no set AQ signature was observed among different Large aggregates. A consistent shift of abundance from HHQ to PQS was observed over time in these Large aggregates. Notably, Large aggregates were spatially co-located with regions void of living cells, suggesting that cell death is associated with the presence of Large aggregates, especially in the Interior region. Interestingly, the outer portion of these Large aggregates stained positive for lipids while the centers, associated with cell death, did not. One intriguing hypothesis for the correlation between cell death and relative paucity of lipids at the aggregate centers focuses on the release of lipase from outer membrane vesicles that degrade interior lipids prior to imaging ( 41 ). At points where Large aggregates intersect with the Ring, protrusions rich in extracellular DNA extend into the Outer region of the biofilm. These protrusions lacked living cells and resembled dispersion events that have been observed in flow-cell biofilm environments ( 39 , 41 ). Large, Medium, and rays of the Small aggregates exhibited crystalline structures that required the PQS pathway for their formation. The mixed birefringence signatures of these aggregates suggest that the constituent biomolecules assemble into a condensed form, similar to biomolecular condensates that have been observed to spatially organize intracellular spaces ( 35 , 37 ). The spatiotemporal appearance of these birefringent aggregates allows differentiation within the biofilm and serves as an additional level of AQ regulation. The role of condensates at the biofilm level deserves greater attention and thorough examination. Thus, AQs are strongly associated with acellular aggregates that differentiate regions of the biofilm and provide an additional level of metabolite regulation. Within the different aggregate types, AQs are associated with distinct behaviors, for example, some cause cell death while others do not. In the absence of AQs cell death was more spatially homogeneous and increased, indicating an important role for AQs in community fitness, possibly through controlled autolysis supporting horizontal gene transfer, the elimination of damaged cells, and dispersal of nutrients to surviving cells. The aggregate formation, birefringence, and levels of cell death observed here were not mediated by Pf4 bacteriophage and are therefore likely due to the AQs themselves. While more research is needed to better understand aggregate formation, the multimodal structural and chemical imaging presented here shows that AQs drive heterogeneity by promoting the formation of aggregates that differentiate microbial communities and improve survival in P. aeruginosa biofilms.

MATERIALS AND METHODS.content: Bacterial strains and plasmids used in this study are listed in Table S2. P. aeruginosa strains were routinely grown from frozen stocks on LB agar plates. Strains were cultured overnight in modified FAB containing 30 mM glucose ( 59 ) for 18 hours. One microliter of culture was used to inoculate modified FAB plates containing 12 mM glucose and 1.5% Noble agar. The inoculation was allowed to dry and inverted plates were incubated at 37°C for 24, 48, or 72 hours. PI at 20 mg/mL was added to media at either 4 or 8 uL/100 mL for the detection of extracellular DNA at high and low magnification, respectively. Nile red at 1 mg/mL Nile red (in 85% propylene glycol and 0.45 um filter sterile) was added at 1 mL per 100 mL. Samples for whole biofilm images were prepared as described above with the modifications of using 12 mL of nutrient agar and curing with lids off. Deletion of pqsA was achieved by conjugational mating using pEXG2-pqsA-KO ( 60 ). Deletion of intF4 was achieved by conjugation of pEX-Δ0728 ( 49 ). Merodiploids were selected on Vogel-Bonner minimal medium (VBMM) agar plates ( 61 ) containing 100 µg/mL gentamicin. Double crossover recombinants were selected on 15% wt/vol sucrose LB without NaCl agar plates. Deletions were confirmed by comparing PCR amplification of the pqsA and intF4 regions (using primers listed in Table S3) with that of wild type. PCR products were subsequently sequenced. The pqsA complementation vector was generated using the primer pairs listed in Table S3 to amplify the genomic DNA of pqsA from P. aeruginosa wild type that was cloned by isothermal assembly into pUCP18 to create p. Transformants of pCMV1 in Escherichia coli DH5alpha were cultured in LB medium with ampicillin (100 µg/mL). Plasmid was electroporated into P. aeruginosa using the 10-minute method ( 62 ) and colonies were selected on LB plates with 100 µg/mL carbenicillin. A GFP-expressing version of the ∆pqsA mutant was chromosomally labeled with GFP using a mini-Tn7- gfpmut2 cassette introduced by four-parental mating ( 63 ). Transgenic cells were selected on VBMM agar containing 100 µg/mL gentamicin. Fluorescence was confirmed by microscopy inspection.

Applying CRM coupled with PCA we were able to carry out a comprehensive analysis of the spectra gathered, allowing identification of AQs. Raman spectroscopy cannot discriminate between the AQ congeners with varying alkyl chains, so these AQ signals may include congeners, such as the C-9, in addition to the C-7 congener. Three sets of samples (colony biofilms) were analyzed on three consecutive days and the molecules noted in Fig. 2 were the predominant AQs, identified in a minimum of two of the three samples. Each region was imaged 3–6 times per sample. Aggregate sizes were estimated from measurements of a minimum of 30 total structures from at least three separate images. Optical photos of the biofilms were acquired using a Nikon D3300 camera (Nikon, Melville, NY) with an 18–55 mm zoom f/3.5-5.6G VR II lens. Fluorescence microscopy of bacterial biofilms in Fig. 3 was obtained using a 90i upright Nikon A1confocal microscope equipped with a 100× LU Plan Fluor objective with simultaneous excitation at 488 nm and 561 nm with emission capture using settings of 525 nm ± 50 nm and 595 nm ± 50 nm. Figure 4a through c was obtained using a Leica Stellaris 8 with a 10× objective NA 0.4 and excitation/emission of 488/493-556 and 561/588-72. This image is a z stack of 156 microns (image presented as a max intensity projection) and image processing was carried out using LAS X version 4/6.1.27508 software. Fluorescence, brightfield, and polarized light microscopy of colony biofilms were performed using a Keyence BZ-X810 microscope equipped with 4× (NA 0.13) and 10× (NA 0.3) objective lenses. The GFP signal was detected from 495 to 545 nm on the visible light spectrum (Chroma filter set 49002) and PI and Nile red were detected from 590 to 665 nm (Chroma filter set 49008). Polarizing lenses above and below the sample were used for polarized light images. A minimum of three colony biofilms were analyzed on two separate days for all-optical microscopy. Images were processed for presentation using BZ-X800 Analyzer software, version 1.1.1.8. Comparison of the ΔintF4 mutant to wild type was carried out using a Leica DM6 B microscope and 5× (NA 0.14) objective. Mean fluorescence was obtained using an identical microscopy setting and quantified using ImageJ analysis software ( 64 ). Colony biofilms incubated for 48 hours were washed for 30 minutes with 0.2% sodium dodecyl sulfate before fluorescence imaging. Graphs were assembled using GraphPad Prism 10 software and error was determined using a two-tailed t -test. For localization of AQs to aggregates, CRM measurements were performed using an Alpha 300R confocal Raman microscope coupled to a 532 nm laser (WITec, Germany) on non-fluorescent bacterial strains. CRM data were analyzed by PCA using an in-house algorithm performed with the Bioinformatics toolbox software in MATLAB using modified scripts from previously published work ( 65 ). A minimum of five structures were analyzed from a colony biofilm on three separate days. Representative images are presented in this work. Principal component scores were plotted to obtain the number of statistically significant principal components (usually 5 or 6), and heat maps for each PC were generated. Subrange plots were performed by selecting regions of interest.
